The **mung bean**, sometimes simply called **moong**,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) is a variety of pulse.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The beans are small and round to oval in shape.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) The outer skin is green, while the flesh is white to light yellow.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Mung beans can be purchased fresh, dried, and dried before splitting into dal.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) It may also be available powdered.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:Mung_bean_sprouts.jpg)

Mung bean sprouts

Like other pulses, mung beans can be cooked for serving whole,[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) such as by simmering in water. When cooked in this way, they become tender and mildly sweet,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) and they can then be used in both sweet and savory preparations, especially in East and South Asian cuisines.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Mung beans can also be sprouted into bean sprouts or refined into mung starch,[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) which can be made into products like glass noodles.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)
